title: Individual Differences: Personality, Perception & Attitudes
simple_html:
People differ. Two employees in the same job, with the same pay and the same boss, can behave completely differently. This lesson explains the three big sources of those differences: personality (stable traits that shape how we typically act), perception (how we interpret the world, which is often biased), and attitudes (our evaluations of things, like how satisfied we are with our job).

Personality

The most accepted model is the Big Five: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (remember it as OCEAN). Of these, conscientiousness (being organized and dependable) is the best single predictor of job performance across almost all jobs.

Perception

We don't see reality directly; we interpret it. That interpretation is shaped by shortcuts and biases. A key one is the fundamental attribution error — we blame other people's failures on their character but excuse our own as bad luck.

Attitudes

An attitude is a positive or negative evaluation. The most studied work attitude is job satisfaction. Dissatisfied employees may quit, complain, slack off, or simply stay loyal and wait for things to improve.

Key takeaways

  • The Big Five (OCEAN) is the standard personality framework; conscientiousness predicts performance best.
  • Perception is interpretation, not photography — and it is full of biases.
  • The fundamental attribution error makes us judge others harshly and ourselves kindly.
  • Job satisfaction is the most important work attitude and affects performance, turnover, and citizenship.
  • Managers who understand individual differences can place, motivate, and coach people more effectively.
intermediate_html:
Individual differences explain why people in identical situations respond differently. This lesson covers three interlocking topics — personality, perception, and attitudes — each of which feeds directly into motivation, leadership, and team behavior later in the course.

Personality and the Big Five

Personality is the stable set of characteristics that shape how a person consistently thinks, feels, and behaves. The dominant model is the Five-Factor Model (Big Five / OCEAN):

TraitHigh scorers are...Work relevance
OpennessCurious, creativeAdapt to change, creative roles
ConscientiousnessOrganized, dependableBest single predictor of performance
ExtraversionSociable, assertiveSales, leadership emergence
AgreeablenessCooperative, trustingTeamwork, service roles
NeuroticismAnxious, moody (low = emotional stability)Stress tolerance, satisfaction

Other relevant traits include core self-evaluation (overall self-worth and capability), locus of control (internal = "I control my outcomes" vs. external = "luck/others control them"), and self-efficacy (belief in one's ability to do a specific task). The MBTI is popular in practice but lacks the predictive validity of the Big Five.

Perception and attribution

Perception is the process of selecting, organizing, and interpreting sensory input to give meaning to the environment. Because we cannot process everything, we use shortcuts that introduce systematic error. Attribution theory explains how we assign causes to behavior, judging whether it is internal (disposition) or external (situation) based on consensus, distinctiveness, and consistency. Two pervasive biases dominate: the fundamental attribution error (overweighting personality and underweighting situation when judging others) and the self-serving bias (crediting our successes to ourselves and blaming failures on circumstances).

Attitudes and job satisfaction

An attitude has three components — cognitive (belief), affective (feeling), and behavioral (intention). The most studied is job satisfaction; related attitudes are job involvement and organizational commitment. When attitudes and behaviors clash, people experience cognitive dissonance (Festinger, 1957) and adjust one to reduce the discomfort. Hirschman's EVLN model describes four responses to dissatisfaction: Exit (quit), Voice (speak up constructively), Loyalty (wait optimistically), and Neglect (let things worsen).

Worked example

A manager, Sara, notices that an analyst, Omar, missed a deadline. Her instinct is "Omar is lazy" (fundamental attribution error — a dispositional judgment). Applying attribution theory, Sara checks consensus (did others also miss it? yes — high consensus suggests an external cause), distinctiveness (does Omar usually deliver? yes — high distinctiveness), and consistency (is this a one-off? yes — low consistency). All three point to a situational cause: the data feed was late. Sara reframes the problem, fixes the process, and preserves Omar's engagement and job satisfaction. Had she acted on the bias, she might have damaged his attitude and triggered Exit or Neglect.

How to apply it

  1. Use validated personality measures (Big Five) for selection and team composition, not pop instruments alone.
  2. Slow down attributions: ask whether the situation, not the person, explains behavior.
  3. Monitor job satisfaction and respond to early Voice signals before they become Exit.
  4. Build self-efficacy through mastery experiences and supportive feedback.
full_html:
Individual differences are the foundation of the individual level of analysis. They explain variance in behavior that situations alone cannot, and they recur throughout the course: personality shapes leadership emergence and team roles; perception underlies decision-making bias and conflict; attitudes drive performance, turnover, and citizenship. This lesson integrates three literatures — personality, perception and attribution, and attitudes — with their seminal sources, debates, and applications.

Personality: structure and prediction

Personality refers to the enduring patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving that distinguish individuals. The field converged in the late twentieth century on the Five-Factor Model (Costa & McCrae, 1992; Goldberg, 1990), summarized as OCEAN: Openness to experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (its inverse is emotional stability). Meta-analytic work established that conscientiousness is the most consistent dispositional predictor of job performance across occupations (Barrick & Mount, 1991), while extraversion predicts leadership emergence and performance in social roles. The Big Five is grounded in the lexical hypothesis — that important individual differences become encoded in language.

Beyond the Big Five, several narrower constructs matter for OB. Core self-evaluation (Judge, Locke & Durham, 1997) is a higher-order trait combining self-esteem, generalized self-efficacy, internal locus of control, and emotional stability; it predicts job satisfaction and performance. Locus of control (Rotter, 1966) distinguishes internals (who believe they control outcomes) from externals (who attribute outcomes to luck or powerful others). Self-efficacy (Bandura, 1977) is the task-specific belief in one's capability and is built through mastery, modeling, persuasion, and arousal management. The Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) remains popular for development and self-awareness but is widely criticized for poor test-retest reliability and weak predictive validity; OB scholars favor the Big Five for selection. A darker cluster — the Dark Triad (narcissism, Machiavellianism, psychopathy; Paulhus & Williams, 2002) — predicts counterproductive and unethical behavior and resurfaces in the leadership lessons.

Perception and attribution

Perception is the cognitive process of selecting, organizing, and interpreting stimuli into a meaningful picture. Because attention and memory are limited, perception relies on schemas and heuristics that systematically distort. Attribution theory (Heider, 1958; Kelley, 1967) specifies how observers infer whether behavior is internally (dispositional) or externally (situational) caused, using three cues: consensus (do others behave similarly?), distinctiveness (does the person behave differently across situations?), and consistency (does the behavior recur over time?). Two robust biases follow. The fundamental attribution error (Ross, 1977) is the tendency to overattribute others' behavior to disposition and underweight situational forces. The self-serving bias attributes one's own successes internally and failures externally, protecting self-esteem. Other perceptual shortcuts include the halo effect, stereotyping, contrast effects, and projection — each capable of corrupting performance appraisal and selection. These biases connect directly to the decision-making concepts (anchoring, heuristics and biases) treated elsewhere in the course.

Attitudes, satisfaction, and dissonance

An attitude is an evaluative statement — favorable or unfavorable — about an object, person, or event, traditionally decomposed into cognitive, affective, and behavioral components. The most researched work attitude is job satisfaction; alongside it sit job involvement, psychological empowerment, and organizational commitment (affective, continuance, and normative; Meyer & Allen, 1991). A foundational mechanism is Festinger's (1957) cognitive dissonance: when attitudes and behaviors are inconsistent, the resulting discomfort motivates people to change an attitude, change the behavior, or rationalize the inconsistency. The strength of the attitude-behavior link increases with attitude specificity, accessibility, social pressure, and direct experience (Ajzen & Fishbein, 1980).

Responses to dissatisfaction are captured by Hirschman's (1970) EVLN typology: Exit (leaving), Voice (active, constructive attempts to improve conditions), Loyalty (passively waiting for improvement), and Neglect (passively allowing deterioration through absenteeism or reduced effort). Satisfaction also predicts organizational citizenship behavior and customer satisfaction, while dissatisfaction predicts withdrawal and counterproductive behavior. The classic "happy-productive worker" claim, however, is only modestly supported, and the causal arrow is contested.

Worked example

A retail chain finds that one store has high turnover. Diagnosis spans all three topics. Personality/selection: the store over-hired low-conscientiousness staff into a process-heavy role — a person-job misfit. Perception: the manager, exhibiting the fundamental attribution error, labels leavers as "unmotivated" rather than examining scheduling and support. Attitudes: pulse surveys show low affective commitment and rising Neglect (absenteeism) before Exit. The evidence-based response combines better selection (Big Five screening for conscientiousness and emotional stability), manager training to counter attributional bias, and acting on Voice signals to raise satisfaction. This integrates the lesson's three strands into one intervention.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • Trait versus situation. The person-situation debate (Mischel, 1968) cautions that traits predict aggregated, not single-instance, behavior; strong situations suppress trait expression.
  • Cross-cultural validity. The Big Five replicates broadly but trait meaning and the salience of MBTI-style typologies vary culturally (Hofstede, 1980).
  • Overusing weak instruments. Treating MBTI results as fixed selection criteria is a common error; it lacks predictive validity.
  • Attribution traps in appraisal. Halo effects and the fundamental attribution error distort performance reviews; structured, behavior-based ratings mitigate them.
  • Assuming satisfaction causes performance. The relationship is reciprocal and modest, not a simple lever.

Cross-links

This lesson sits between Foundations of OB (Lesson 1) and the motivation lessons (3-4): personality and self-efficacy feed expectancy and goal-setting theory; perception underlies the decision-making biases; and attitudes (satisfaction, commitment) are the outcomes that motivation, leadership (Lessons 7-8), and culture (Lesson 9) ultimately aim to improve. Self-efficacy and core self-evaluation also reappear in the leadership and stress literatures.
